response: The global food system faces unprecedented stress from population growth, climate change, resource limits, and waste. By 2050 the world is projected to reach 9.7 billion people, requiring about a 60% increase in food production, while climate impacts are already cutting yields via droughts, floods, and extreme temperatures; agriculture also accounts for roughly 25% of greenhouse gas emissions. Multiple constraints—such as water scarcity (including faster-than-recharge depletion of the Ogallala Aquifer), soil degradation reducing productivity on 33% of the world’s arable land, and food waste of about one-third of all food—further worsen the outlook. Proposed responses include precision agriculture, vertical farming, alternative proteins, drought-resistant genetics, and regenerative farming, alongside policy changes to enable large-scale implementation while keeping food affordable and accessible.
